compiled_state.author: Iso Tester
compiled_state.description:
The completion-verification close gate's escape hatch `--force-close="<reason>"` is reachable from the CLI but NOT from the Python library API: `rebar.transition(...)` (`src/rebar/__init__.py:125`, the `def` line) exposes only `force`, not `force_close`. A programmatic client that hits the close gate with LLM deps absent therefore has no in-library bypass and must shell out to the CLI — a real client-facing reliability/UX gap.

## Why
Library, CLI, and MCP are meant to be behaviorally at parity over one store (`tests/interfaces/facades/test_parity.py`). The close-gate bypass is an asymmetry that breaks that contract for library consumers.

## What / files (verified against current code)
The command layer ALREADY implements `force_close` end-to-end — `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` `_parse_flags` extracts `--force-close` (lines 107/131/136), and `transition_compute(..., force_close: str = "")` (lines 185/316) honors it on the close path (lines 214/463-470: a forced close skips BOTH the completion verifier and signing, leaving `signature: null`). The CLI already maps `--force-close` here. So the ONLY missing wiring is the library wrapper:
- `src/rebar/__init__.py` — add a `force_close: str | None = None` parameter to the `transition()` wrapper and thread it into the existing `_commands` call (the same call that already passes `force`). Map `None`→`""` to match the command-layer default. Do NOT duplicate or re-implement any gate logic; just pass the value through to the seam that already honors it. Positive rationale: the command layer is the single source of truth for the bypass; the wrapper's only job is to expose the existing parameter so library consumers reach the same seam as the CLI.

## Parity test
- The shared `Adapter` protocol (`tests/interfaces/adapters.py:76`) declares `transition(self, tid, current, target)` with no `force_close`, and `LibraryAdapter`/`CliAdapter`/`McpAdapter` mirror it. Add a FOCUSED parity test (not a change to the generic protocol's existing call sites) that drives a force-close through BOTH the library (`rebar.transition(id, "in_progress", "closed", force_close="reason")`) and the CLI (`rebar transition id in_progress closed --force-close=reason`) under `require_completion_verification_for_close=true`, and asserts identical outcome: ticket reaches `closed` AND `signature is None` (the closed-without-signature signal) in both. Keep `tests/interfaces/facades/test_parity.py` untouched if extending it is invasive — a new focused test module is acceptable.
- Proving command: `pytest <the new parity test> -q` (named in the verify commands).

## Scope
In: library `transition(force_close=...)` parity + the focused parity test. Out: changing gate semantics, the CLI surface (already correct), the command-layer `transition.py` (already honors `force_close`), and MCP. Non-happy-path input validation is out — mirror the CLI exactly (a non-empty reason string is required by the CLI's `--force-close=`; the library passes the string through unchanged).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `rebar.transition(id, "in_progress", "closed", force_close="reason")` closes a ticket under `require_completion_verification_for_close=true` WITHOUT running the verifier, mirroring the CLI (the command layer already honors it; only the wrapper param is added).
- [ ] The forced close records the same closed-without-signature signal as the CLI path (`signature: null`).
- [ ] A focused parity test asserts CLI and library force-close behave identically (closed + `signature is None`); it is the named proving command (`pytest …`).
- [ ] No change to the non-forced close path; the existing `test_parity.py` parity suite still passes unchanged.
- [ ] `make check` passes.
compiled_state.title: Library/CLI parity: add force_close to rebar.transition
